title: GCP Professional Cloud Architect — Case Study Edition
description: 50 case study questions based on the 4 official GCP PCA case studies: Mountkirk Games (gaming), Dress4Win (retail), TerramEarth (IoT), and Helix (healthcare). Tests migration, architecture decisions, and real-world scenarios.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games needs to build a real-time leaderboard for 50 million players with sub-100ms global latency and support for 1 million concurrent writes per second during peak events. Which GCP database architecture?

choices.text: Cloud Spanner with multi-region (nam-eur) configuration — global writes, strong consistency, 99.999% SLA but Cloud Spanner's write throughput is limited by distributed transaction overhead at 1M writes/sec scale

Cloud Bigtable as the primary write store with autoscaling, Cloud Memorystore for Redis (replicated) for in-memory leaderboard cache, and Dataproc for nightly batch recalculation of historical rankings
Firestore in multi-region mode for leaderboard writes + Cloud Spanner for historical game state — Firestore writes 1M/sec is achievable with sharding, Spanner for relational data
GKE with Cassandra on persistent SSD — Cassandra handles 1M writes/sec but requires significant operational overhead managing the cluster

questions.explanation: Cloud Bigtable handles 1M+ writes/sec with horizontal scaling, integrates with Dataproc for batch reprocessing, and Cloud Memorystore Redis provides sub-millisecond read latency for the active leaderboard. This is the standard pattern for gaming leaderboards on GCP.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games is designing a game analytics pipeline: 500GB of gameplay events per hour from globally distributed game servers, processed in real-time for live game ops dashboards, and archived for 90 days. Which data pipeline?

choices.text: Pub/Sub (global) → Dataflow streaming (windowed aggregation) → BigQuery (real-time) + Cloud Storage (archive with 90-day lifecycle) — Pub/Sub handles global ingestion, Dataflow provides exactly-once processing semantics
Pub/Sub Lite (zonal) → Dataflow streaming → BigQuery (real-time) — Pub/Sub Lite is cheaper but only zonal, not suitable for global game event ingestion
Cloud Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions → BigQuery — Cloud Functions can't handle 500GB/hour sustained throughput, not designed for streaming
Apache Kafka on GKE (Confluent) → Dataproc Streaming → BigQuery — self-managed Kafka on GKE adds significant operational overhead for what Pub/Sub handles natively

questions.explanation: Pub/Sub's global endpoints handle geographically distributed game server ingestion. Dataflow streaming with exactly-once semantics prevents duplicate event processing. BigQuery streaming inserts enable real-time dashboards. Cloud Storage archive with lifecycle policy handles the 90-day retention. This is the standard GCP gaming analytics pattern.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games needs to auto-scale their game server backend (stateful, 50 players per server instance) based on concurrent player count, with scale-out in under 60 seconds. Which GKE configuration?

choices.text: GKE Autopilot with custom metric (concurrent players) via Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) — Autopilot manages nodes, HPA scales pods based on custom metric from Cloud Monitoring
GKE Standard with Managed Instance Groups (MIG) — MIGs are for GCE, not GKE pods
GKE with Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) — VPA adjusts pod resource requests, not pod count for scaling
Compute Engine MIG with autoscaler based on game server CPU — lift-and-shift approach, but GKE is more cloud-native for containerized game servers

questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot + HPA with custom metric (concurrent players via Cloud Monitoring custom metric API) is the most cloud-native approach. HPA scales pods based on actual game load. Autopilot handles node provisioning. Custom metrics require the Stackdriver Adapter for GKE.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games' game clients download game assets (20GB patches) from CDN globally. Which Cloud CDN configuration provides the best cache hit ratio and lowest origin load?

choices.text: Cloud CDN with backend bucket pointing to Cloud Storage (multi-region) + cache key includes query string + signed URLs for premium users — signed URLs prevent unauthorized sharing
Cloud CDN with backend bucket pointing to Cloud Storage + enable CDN cache invalidation for urgent patches + set long TTL (7 days) for static game assets + signed URLs for premium tier
Cloud CDN with compute engine backend (VMs serving files) + Cloud Storage as backup — GCE as origin adds unnecessary complexity, CDN for GCE is legacy
Global external HTTP(S) Load Balancer with Cloud CDN disabled + serve files directly from Cloud Storage — this bypasses CDN caching benefits

questions.explanation: Cloud CDN with Cloud Storage backend bucket is the standard pattern for static game asset delivery. Long TTLs (7 days) for immutable game assets maximize cache hit ratio. Cache invalidation for urgent patches ensures fast propagation. Signed URLs for premium users prevent unauthorized sharing without separate authentication infrastructure.

questions.question: Dress4Win is migrating a 3-tier .NET application (IIS web servers, Windows services, MS SQL Server 2016) from on-premises to GCP. They have 50 web server VMs, 10 app server VMs, and 2 SQL Server VMs (4TB). Which migration approach?

choices.text: Migrate web servers to GKE with Windows containers (Windows node pools) + SQL Server to Cloud SQL for SQL Server with HA + app servers as Windows containers on GKE
Lift-and-shift all 62 VMs to Compute Engine — preserve Windows Server tooling, use Managed Instance Groups for web servers, Cloud SQL for database
Re-architect to ASP.NET Core on Cloud Run + Cloud SQL + GKE for Windows services — Cloud Run doesn't support Windows containers
Migrate to Azure App Service for web tier, Azure SQL Database for SQL Server — wrong cloud for GCP exam scenario

questions.explanation: GKE with Windows node pools is the migration path for Windows .NET apps on IIS. Windows containers on GKE (Windows Server container support) preserve the IIS configuration while enabling container orchestration. Cloud SQL for SQL Server with HA replaces the on-prem SQL with managed database. This is a lift-and-shift containerization.

questions.question: Dress4Win needs to migrate their CI/CD pipeline from Jenkins (on-premises) to GCP. Their pipeline builds .NET containers, runs unit tests, scans for vulnerabilities, and deploys to GKE. Which GCP-native CI/CD?

choices.text: Cloud Build for build/test/scanning + Artifact Registry for container images + Cloud Deploy for GKE progressive delivery + Binary Authorization to enforce signed image policy before GKE deployment
GitLab CI on Compute Engine — migrate Jenkins to GCE, keep GitLab CI as the runner, simpler migration path but not GCP-native
Jenkins on GKE with Helm — migrate Jenkins to GKE, Jenkins X is the Kubernetes-native evolution but requires re-architecting pipelines
Cloud Build + Jenkins shared library — Cloud Build orchestrates Jenkins, Jenkins runs build steps, too complex for migration

questions.explanation: Cloud Build + Artifact Registry + Cloud Deploy + Binary Authorization is the fully GCP-managed replacement for Jenkins. Cloud Build handles build/test/vulnerability scanning (Container Analysis). Cloud Deploy manages progressive delivery to GKE. Binary Authorization ensures only scanned, signed images deploy. This replaces Jenkins entirely with managed GCP services.

questions.question: Dress4Win's retail website experiences Black Friday traffic spikes: 10x normal traffic for 4 hours. They run 50 web server VMs (2 vCPU, 8GB) on GCE MIGs. Which auto-scaling configuration handles the spike with minimum cost?

choices.text: MIG autoscaler based on CPU > 80%, scale out to 500 VMs, scale in after 10 minutes — standard scaling but 500 VMs for 4 hours is expensive
MIG autoscaler based on HTTP request rate (load balancing metric) + preemptible VMs for 90% of capacity (450 VMs) + standard VMs for 10% (50 VMs) — preemptible handles burst at 91% discount
Cloud Run for the web application — scales to zero, pay-per-request, automatically handles traffic spikes without pre-provisioning, but requires containerizing the existing .NET app
GKE with HPA based on CPU > 70% — GKE is overkill for simple web servers, adds Kubernetes complexity to a Windows container workload

questions.explanation: MIG autoscaling with preemptible VMs (90% of capacity) gives 91% discount during the 4-hour spike. Standard VMs for baseline capacity. HTTP request rate scaling is more responsive than CPU-based for web servers. This is the most cost-effective approach without refactoring the existing .NET app.

questions.question: Dress4Win needs to store product images (10TB, millions of images) with global fast access and automatic thumbnail generation on upload. Which storage + processing architecture?

choices.text: Cloud Storage (multi-region) for product images + Cloud Functions (trigger on object create) to generate thumbnails and store in a separate Cloud Storage bucket + Cloud CDN for global delivery
Cloud Storage + App Engine Flexible for thumbnail generation — App Engine Flexible has faster scaling than Cloud Functions for burst, but more expensive and unnecessary overhead
Firestore for image storage + Cloud Functions for thumbnails — Firestore is not designed for large binary files like images, should use Cloud Storage
Compute Engine with NFS (Filestore) for images + custom VM-based thumbnail service — Filestore is enterprise NFS, unnecessary overhead for blob storage

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage (multi-region) for the primary images provides global access and durability. Cloud Functions with GCS trigger runs serverless thumbnail generation on upload — pays only for execution. Separate bucket for thumbnails. Cloud CDN caches thumbnails globally. This is the standard GCP image processing pattern.

questions.question: Dress4Win needs to implement a shopping cart that persists across devices and sessions. Cart data is 100KB per user average, needs sub-10ms read/write, and must be globally consistent. Which database?

choices.text: Firestore (in-datacenter mode) — Firestore is multi-region by default, sub-10ms reads, supports offline persistence, ideal for shopping cart
Cloud Bigtable for shopping cart — Bigtable is designed for billions of users, but shopping cart at millions of users is overkill for Bigtable's scale
Cloud SQL for SQL Server with read replica in each region — Cloud SQL is regional, not globally consistent, read replicas are eventually consistent
Cloud Memorystore for Redis — in-memory, sub-ms latency, but no persistence, data lost on restart, unsuitable for cart storage

questions.explanation: Firestore's multi-region deployment provides globally consistent reads and writes with sub-10ms latency in most regions. It handles millions of documents easily. Offline persistence (mobile/web SDK) is a bonus for shopping carts. Redis Memorystore is too volatile for cart data. Bigtable is overkill. Cloud SQL is regional only.

questions.question: TerramEarth has 500,000 vehicles transmitting telemetry every 30 seconds over satellite (high latency, intermittent connectivity, 500 bytes per message). Which IoT ingestion architecture?

choices.text: Cloud IoT Core (deprecated) → Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery — IoT Core was the native GCP IoT ingestion service but has been deprecated, replaced by alternatives
Vehicle gateway sends MQTT to Cloud IoT Core → Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery — IoT Core handles device registry and authentication, but it's been deprecated as of August 2025
Vehicle uses HTTP/REST or MQTT to publish telemetry to Cloud Pub/Sub directly (using service account authentication) → Dataflow streaming → BigQuery for analytics + Cloud Storage for raw archival
Vehicle streams directly to Cloud Bigtable via HBase API — Bigtable ingestion is complex for constrained vehicle hardware, Pub/Sub is simpler

questions.explanation: Vehicles publish directly to Pub/Sub using service account authentication (JSON Web Tokens). This is the modern pattern post-IoT Core deprecation. Dataflow streams to BigQuery for real-time analytics and to Cloud Storage for raw archival. Direct Pub/Sub ingestion is simpler than IoT Core and works over satellite connections with HTTP/REST.

questions.question: TerramEarth wants to detect anomalies in vehicle sensor data (temperature, pressure, RPM) in real-time and alert field technicians within 60 seconds. Which real-time analytics architecture?

choices.text: Pub/Sub → Dataflow streaming with anomaly detection (using a machine learning model or threshold-based rules) → Cloud Pub/Sub output topic → Cloud Functions (trigger) → SMS/email via third-party (Twilio/SendGrid) to field technicians
Pub/Sub → BigQuery streaming insert → scheduled query runs every 60 seconds to detect anomalies → Cloud Monitoring alerting → notify via email/SMS
Pub/Sub Lite → Dataflow → BigQuery → Cloud Logging with log-based alert — BigQuery is not real-time enough for sub-minute anomaly detection
Cloud IoT Core → Cloud Functions → Cloud Monitoring → alerting — IoT Core is deprecated, Cloud Functions has cold start latency

questions.explanation: Dataflow streaming with anomaly detection processes sensor data as it arrives, immediately publishing anomalies to a Pub/Sub topic that triggers Cloud Functions for notifications. This achieves <60 second alert latency. BigQuery streaming + scheduled query introduces too much latency.

questions.question: TerramEarth needs to store 5 years of vehicle telemetry (100TB/day = 180PB total) for regulatory compliance and historical analytics. Which storage architecture with cost optimization?

choices.text: Cloud Storage (Standard) for all 180PB — Standard tier is expensive at this scale, should tier the data based on age and access frequency
BigQuery for all telemetry — BigQuery is for structured analytical queries, not raw telemetry storage; 100TB/day into BigQuery is cost-prohibitive, better for processed/aggregated data

Cloud Storage with lifecycle: ingest to Standard → 90 days Nearline → 1 year Coldline → 5 years Archive. Use BigQuery for processed aggregates from Cloud Storage. Store raw data in Cloud Storage Parquet format for cost-efficient analytics via BigQuery external queries.
Cloud Bigtable for all 180PB — Bigtable can scale to this but is expensive for rarely-accessed historical data and doesn't support SQL queries directly

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage with tiered lifecycle + BigQuery for analytics is the standard pattern for IoT time-series data at scale. Raw data stored in Parquet on Cloud Storage (external table in BigQuery). Lifecycle: Standard (90 days) → Nearline (1 year) → Coldline (4 years) → Archive (compliant 5-year retention). 180PB at Standard rates would be $3.2M/month; lifecycle tiers reduce this significantly.

questions.question: TerramEarth vehicles need to receive firmware updates over-the-air (OTA). Updates are 500MB each, applied to 500K vehicles, with guaranteed delivery and rollback capability. Which update architecture?

choices.text: Cloud Storage (multi-region) with signed URLs for each vehicle + Cloud Functions to track delivery status in Firestore + vehicle-side update agent that reports status back to Firestore

Cloud Storage + Cloud Pub/Sub for update notifications + Dataproc for firmware image processing + Firestore to track vehicle update state machine (pending → downloading → applying → complete/failed) + vehicle reports to Firestore

Cloud Storage (signed URLs) + Pub/Sub for update notifications + Firestore for vehicle state tracking (500K documents) + vehicle-side agent downloads from GCS signed URL, reports status to Firestore + rollback via previous firmware version stored in GCS
Cloud CDN for firmware distribution + Pub/Sub Lite for notifications — CDN provides edge caching for efficient large file delivery, Pub/Sub Lite handles the high-volume vehicle notifications

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage signed URLs let vehicles download firmware directly (GCS handles CDN edge delivery globally). Firestore tracks the state machine for each of 500K vehicles (downloading → applying → complete/failed). Previous firmware version is stored in GCS for rollback. This is the standard OTA update pattern on GCP.

questions.question: TerramEarth wants to use ML to predict vehicle maintenance needs (predictive maintenance) based on 2 years of historical sensor data (500TB). Which ML training and serving architecture?

choices.text: BigQuery ML with logistic regression — run ML training directly on data in BigQuery, no data movement, simple SQL interface, sufficient for predictive maintenance classification
Dataproc Serverless for Spark MLlib — upload 500TB from Cloud Storage to Dataproc, train with Spark MLlib, deploy model to Vertex AI — data movement is expensive and time-consuming
Vertex AI AutoML for tabular data — upload training data to Vertex AI datasets, AutoML trains the model automatically, deploy to Vertex AI endpoints for real-time predictions
Train model on-premises, export to TensorFlow SavedModel, deploy to Vertex AI Prediction — unnecessary given data is already in GCP

questions.explanation: BigQuery ML with the data already in BigQuery (ingested from Cloud Storage via Dataflow) is the lowest-friction approach for predictive maintenance. Logistic regression or gradient boosted trees in BQML handle time-series predictive maintenance well. No data movement needed. Vertex AI AutoML is also valid but BQML is simpler for SQL-experienced data engineers.

questions.question: Helix needs to process and store genomic data (100TB) for clinical research. Data contains PHI and must comply with HIPAA. Researchers need to query the data for clinical trials. Which storage and access architecture?

choices.text: Cloud Storage with CMEK (Cloud KMS) + VPC Service Controls + DLP API to classify PHI fields + BigQuery with authorized views for researchers + Cloud Audit Logs (Data Access) for HIPAA compliance
Cloud Storage with Google-managed encryption + IAM-only access control + Cloud Audit Logs — Google-managed keys don't meet HIPAA requirement for customer-managed keys
Filestore (enterprise NFS) for genomic data + IAM controls — Filestore doesn't support the audit and compliance requirements for PHI
BigQuery with customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) only — BigQuery alone doesn't provide the VPC perimeter and DLP that HIPAA requires for genomic data

questions.explanation: HIPAA compliance on GCP requires: CMEK via Cloud KMS (HIPAA requires encryption with customer-managed keys), VPC Service Controls (data perimeter preventing exfiltration), Cloud DLP API for PHI classification, BigQuery authorized views for row/column-level access, and Cloud Audit Logs with Data Access logs enabled for the audit trail.

questions.question: Helix needs to share clinical genomic data with 5 partner research institutions globally. Each partner has their own identity provider. Data sharing must be HIPAA-compliant and partners must not be able to download raw PHI. Which architecture?

choices.text: Create external identities in Helix's Google Cloud for each partner user (B2B collaboration) + grant BigQuery authorized views (no raw data export) + VPC Service Controls to enforce data perimeter — partners authenticate via their IdP through federation
Create service accounts for each partner, store keys in partner systems — service account keys are a security risk and don't federate with partner IdPs
Export data to partner cloud storage buckets (GCS/S3) — exporting PHI to external clouds violates HIPAA data handling requirements without additional controls
Use Cloud Storage signed URLs for data access — signed URLs give full access to the underlying data, not suitable for PHI sharing

questions.explanation: External Identities (B2B federation) lets partner users authenticate with their own IdP (Okta, Azure AD, etc.) without creating Google accounts. BigQuery authorized views allow queries but prevent raw data export. VPC Service Controls ensure data can't leave the security perimeter. This is HIPAA-compliant federated data access on GCP.

questions.question: Helix's clinical application runs on GKE with sensitive patient data in Cloud SQL. During a security audit, they discover that a compromised container could potentially read from Cloud SQL using the node's service account. How do you prevent this?

choices.text: Enable Workload Identity on GKE — binds the Kubernetes Service Account (KSA) to the GCP Service Account (GSA). Pods use the KSA, which is automatically mapped to the GSA with minimal permissions. Node service account is never used by pods.
Create a dedicated service account per application with minimal RBAC permissions — service accounts are shared at node level, not per-pod, so this doesn't prevent the attack
Use Cloud IAP (Identity-Aware Proxy) to tunnel all database access — IAP is for human access via the proxy, not container-to-database traffic
Deploy a sidecar proxy (Istio/Anthos Service Mesh) to intercept database calls — adds operational complexity, Workload Identity is the simpler solution

questions.explanation: Workload Identity is the Google-recommended solution for this exact attack vector. It eliminates the need for service account keys on nodes entirely. Pods authenticate as the GCP service account via the KSA-GSA binding. The node's default service account is never used by application pods. This prevents a compromised container from using the node's service account.

questions.question: Helix needs to archive clinical trial documents (50TB, long-term, must be tamper-proof) for 25 years. Documents must be retrievable within 1 hour for audit purposes. Which Cloud Storage configuration?

choices.text: Cloud Storage with Archive storage class + Object Versioning (enabled) + retention policy (25 years) + bucket lock (immutability policy) + Cloud KMS for customer-managed keys + archival metadata — Archive retrieval is 12 hours by default, not 1 hour

Cloud Storage Nearline (for 1-hour access) with retention policy of 25 years + Object Lock (WORM) + CMEK via Cloud KMS + bucket policy-only access — Nearline is more expensive than Coldline/Archive but meets 1-hour RTO

Cloud Storage Nearline + Object Versioning + 25-year Object Lock (immutability) + CMEK + Cloud DNS for retrieval — 1-hour retrieval is possible with Nearline, Coldline is 12 hours, Archive is 12+ hours

Cloud Storage Coldline with 25-year Object Lock (WORM/immutability) + CMEK + retention policy + lifecycle to Archive after 10 years — Coldline retrieval is 12 hours, NOT 1 hour. For 1-hour retrieval, Nearline is required.

questions.explanation: Coldline has 12-hour retrieval SLA, not 1-hour. For 1-hour access + 25-year retention + immutability + CMEK, Nearline is the appropriate tier (1-hour retrieval SLA). Archive has 12+ hour retrieval SLA. Object Lock enforces immutability (WORM) for HIPAA-required tamper-proof storage. CMEK via Cloud KMS provides encryption with customer-managed keys.

questions.question: Helix needs to implement column-level encryption for a PHI database in Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL: patient_id and diagnosis columns must be encrypted with keys managed by Helix (not Google). Which implementation?

choices.text: Use pgcrypto extension in PostgreSQL with keys stored in Cloud KMS — pgcrypto provides column-level encryption, Cloud KMS stores encryption keys, application handles key retrieval from KMS
Cloud SQL with CMEK — CMEK encrypts the entire disk, not individual columns, patient_id and diagnosis are still readable at the column level
Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in Cloud SQL — TDE encrypts the entire database at the storage layer, not column-level, and keys are managed by Google, not Helix
Application-level encryption before inserting to Cloud SQL — encrypt patient_id and diagnosis in the application code using Cloud KMS, store encrypted blobs in Cloud SQL

questions.explanation: PostgreSQL's pgcrypto extension with Cloud KMS-backed keys is the correct approach for column-level encryption with customer-managed keys. The application retrieves the key from Cloud KMS and uses pgcrypto to encrypt/decrypt specific columns. CMEK and TDE are disk-level encryption, not column-level.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games is deploying a new game mode globally. They need to test with 5% of users in production (canary) with the ability to instantly roll back if error rate spikes. Which deployment + traffic splitting strategy?

choices.text: Cloud Deploy with GKE progressive delivery: deploy new game server version, set canary percentage to 5%, monitor Cloud Monitoring error rate, auto-rollback if error rate > 1%, promote to 100% after 1 hour stable
Istio VirtualService with weight-based routing (5% to canary) + Prometheus/Grafana for monitoring — requires managing Istio control plane, Cloud Deploy is simpler
Cloud Load Balancer with URL-based routing (5% to canary backend) + manual kubectl rollout — too manual, no automatic rollback
GKE Helm deployment with canary plugin + HPA — canary is based on pod count, not traffic percentage, doesn't support instant rollback

questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy with GKE progressive delivery is the Google-recommended canary deployment. It handles traffic splitting by percentage (not just pod count), integrates with Cloud Monitoring for automatic rollback based on error rates, and supports instant promotion or rollback. This is fully managed with no Istio overhead.

questions.question: Dress4Win needs to migrate 4TB of product catalog data from an on-premises Oracle database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. The migration must have zero downtime and validate data integrity before cutover. Which migration path?

choices.text: Use Database Migration Service (DMS) with online migration (continuous replication) from Oracle to Cloud SQL PostgreSQL — DMS handles schema conversion and continuous sync, validate with checksum comparison, cutover via DNS
Export from Oracle to CSV (expdp), upload to Cloud Storage, import to Cloud SQL PostgreSQL using psql — requires significant downtime for 4TB export/import
Use Data Fusion for CDC-based migration — Data Fusion is ETL-focused, not the best tool for zero-downtime database migration
Use Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL import from Oracle with Ora2Pg — Ora2Pg automates Oracle-to-PostgreSQL schema and data migration, DMS is the managed option

questions.explanation: DMS online migration uses Oracle LogMiner or similar CDC to continuously replicate changes from Oracle to Cloud SQL PostgreSQL. Zero downtime migration with near-real-time sync. Schema conversion is automated. Validation via checksum comparison between source and target. DNS cutover at the end.

questions.question: TerramEarth's fleet management dashboard (web app) shows real-time vehicle positions on a map. It needs to handle 1,000 concurrent users, 10-second position updates per vehicle, and global low-latency access. Which web hosting and data architecture?

choices.text: Cloud Run for the dashboard web app (containerized) + Firestore (real-time) for vehicle positions (Firestore's real-time listeners push updates to web clients) + Cloud CDN for static asset delivery
App Engine Flexible for the dashboard + Cloud Bigtable for vehicle positions — Bigtable's API isn't push-based like Firestore, web clients would need to poll
GKE with a WebSocket-based backend service + Cloud Memorystore for Redis (pub/sub for position updates) + Cloud CDN — too complex, Firestore handles this natively

Firebase Realtime Database + Firebase Hosting — Firebase Hosting serves the dashboard, Realtime Database pushes position updates to web clients via WebSocket. Firebase Realtime Database is deprecated in favor of Firestore.

questions.explanation: Cloud Run serves the dashboard containerized, auto-scales. Firestore's real-time listeners (WebSocket-based) push position updates to web clients every 10 seconds without polling. Cloud CDN caches static assets globally. This is the modern GCP pattern for real-time collaborative apps.

questions.question: Helix needs to send clinical alerts (containing de-identified PHI) via SMS to doctors when patient conditions change. SMS must be delivered within 5 minutes. Which notification architecture?

choices.text: Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions (trigger on alert event) → Twilio SendGrid API for SMS + Cloud Monitoring for SMS delivery tracking — Pub/Sub + Cloud Functions is fully managed, Twilio is the standard for SMS
Cloud Pub/Sub → Cloud Run (HTTP trigger) → Twilio API for SMS — Cloud Run has cold starts that could delay 5-min SLA, Cloud Functions is faster for short-lived triggers
Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) for push notifications to mobile apps — FCM is for mobile app push, not SMS, and doesn't support the HIPAA compliance requirements for clinical alerts
Cloud Scheduler (triggers every minute) → Cloud Functions → Twilio — Cloud Scheduler is for scheduled jobs, not event-driven real-time alerts

questions.explanation: Pub/Sub captures clinical alerts from the processing pipeline. Cloud Functions (1st gen) with Twilio SDK sends SMS immediately on trigger — sub-second cold start, no instance management. Twilio provides SMS delivery confirmation. This achieves <5 minute delivery SLA.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games processes game event data through Dataflow and stores results in BigQuery. During a Dataflow job, a transient network issue caused 15 minutes of events to be lost. How do you prevent this in future runs?

choices.text: Enable Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery with 10-minute deduplication window — Pub/Sub exactly-once prevents duplicate messages and ensures no data loss during transient subscriber failures
Configure Dataflow with checkpointing (stateful processing) — checkpointing saves pipeline state but doesn't prevent upstream data loss from Pub/Sub
Increase Pub/Sub retention duration to 7 days + replay from timestamp when job restarts — this is for job recovery, not prevention of data loss during normal operation
Use Dataflow with a dead-letter topic for failed messages — dead-letter handles failures, not transient network issues that cause data loss

questions.explanation: Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery (enabled with a deduplication window) ensures messages are delivered exactly once even during transient subscriber failures. Combined with Dataflow's built-in exactly-once processing semantics, this prevents both duplicate processing and data loss from network issues.

questions.question: Dress4Win's product images are stored in Cloud Storage with inconsistent naming. They need to standardize naming and generate metadata (dimensions, format, size) for a catalog of 10 million images. Which processing approach?

choices.text: Cloud Functions (GCS trigger) — good for individual object operations, but 10M images × function invocation cost and timeout makes this unsuitable at scale

Dataflow with a custom DoFn to read GCS object list, process each image (ImageMagick/Python Pillow), write metadata to BigQuery + rename objects using GCS Object Compose — batch processing at scale with Dataflow's auto-scaling
Dataproc Serverless for Spark with image processing library — Dataproc Serverless scales automatically, handles 10M images efficiently with distributed processing
Cloud Life Sciences (Google's managed workflow service) + Batch for image processing — Life Sciences is for bioinformatics, Batch is for HPC, Dataflow is the standard GCP ETL tool

questions.explanation: Dataflow batch pipeline reads all 10M objects from GCS (using a ListFiles workflow), processes each image to extract metadata (Pillow/OpenCV), writes metadata to BigQuery, and uses GCS Object Compose API to rename objects. Dataflow's auto-scaling handles the load efficiently. This is the standard GCP pattern for large-scale batch object processing.

questions.question: TerramEarth's data engineering team needs to run ad-hoc SQL queries on vehicle telemetry data in Cloud Storage (Parquet files, 50TB). Queries run once per week. Which query engine is most cost-effective?

choices.text: BigQuery external tables over Cloud Storage Parquet — BigQuery external queries (on-demand) scan only the data needed, $5/TB scanned. For weekly ad-hoc on 50TB, on-demand is cheaper than a persistent cluster.
Dataproc Serverless for Spark SQL — Dataproc Serverless has minimum 10-minute billing, $0.05/vCPU-second minimum, expensive for occasional queries vs BigQuery
Permanent Dataproc cluster (10 nodes) for weekly queries — $0.05/vCPU-hour × 10 × 24 hours × 30 days = $360/month even with no queries, vastly more expensive than BigQuery external
BigQuery persistent table (loaded from Cloud Storage) — loading data into BigQuery costs $0.05/GB, but external tables query data in place without loading cost

questions.explanation: BigQuery external tables query Parquet files in Cloud Storage directly — $5/TB scanned (on-demand). For weekly ad-hoc queries scanning ~50TB/week, this is $250/query × 4 = $1K/month. Dataproc Serverless minimum billing makes it expensive for occasional queries. No persistent cluster needed.

questions.question: Helix needs to manage encryption keys for 500TB of clinical data in Cloud Storage and Cloud SQL. Keys must be rotated annually and rotated immediately if a key compromise is suspected. Which key management?

choices.text: Cloud KMS with automatic key rotation enabled (annual) + Cloud Audit Logs for key access auditing + Cloud KMS import job for externally generated key material if emergency rotation needed
Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) stored in Cloud Storage — CSEK requires manual key management outside GCP, rotation requires re-uploading all data with new key
Cloud HSM (Hardware Security Module) for key storage — HSM provides FIPS 140-2 Level 1 compliance, but Cloud KMS with HSM-backed keys (premium tier) is the GCP equivalent for high-security workloads
Cloud Key Management Vault (Key Vault) — Azure's key management service, not GCP

questions.explanation: Cloud KMS with automatic annual rotation meets the compliance requirement. Cloud Audit Logs capture all key access for compliance auditing. If key compromise is suspected, Cloud KMS supports immediate key rotation (manual) + revocation via key version disable. For HIPAA, HSM-backed KMS keys provide the highest security.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games runs game servers on GKE. During peak, they see packet loss and latency spikes due to network congestion between pods in different availability zones. Which GKE networking optimization?

choices.text: Enable GKE Dataplane V2 (eBPF-based networking) — Dataplane V2 provides network policies and pod-level networking, but doesn't directly address cross-AZ network congestion

Configure GKE node pools with topology-aware routing (enableNodeNa=false) + use Container Network Interface (CNI) with local traffic routing — topology-aware routing ensures pods prefer same-AZ communication
Deploy pods using nodeSelector to pin pods to specific zones — manual placement prevents the orchestrator from optimizing cross-AZ traffic, not recommended
Use a Global load balancer to route traffic to the nearest zone — load balancers handle external traffic, not pod-to-pod traffic within the cluster

questions.explanation: GKE topology-aware routing (enableNodeNa=false in Network Policy config) ensures the Kubernetes scheduler prefers scheduling pods that communicate frequently to the same availability zone. This minimizes cross-AZ network hops and reduces latency. GKE Dataplane V2 improves pod networking but isn't specifically for cross-AZ optimization.

questions.question: Dress4Win is migrating from a monolithic Jenkins CI/CD server to GCP. Their Jenkins has 200 build agents, 50 shared libraries, and complex pipeline scripts. Which migration strategy?

choices.text: Lift-and-shift Jenkins to GCE (Managed Instance Group) — preserve all pipeline scripts and shared libraries, scale agents with MIG, simplest migration path
Migrate to Cloud Build with Jenkins integration (Jenkins Stackdriver Plugin) — Cloud Build becomes the primary CI, Jenkins is still needed for agents, too complex
Rewrite all Jenkins pipelines in Cloud Build YAML — requires rewriting 200 agents worth of pipeline code, too time-consuming for migration
Deploy Jenkins X on GKE for cloud-native Jenkins — Jenkins X is for Kubernetes-native CI/CD, different architecture, requires complete pipeline redesign

questions.explanation: Jenkins on GCE with MIG is the pragmatic migration path: preserves all existing pipelines, shared libraries, and agent configurations. Scale the MIG based on build queue depth. This is a lift-and-shift that eliminates on-premises hardware while maintaining the existing Jenkins investment. Complete rewrite in Cloud Build is too risky for 200 pipelines.

questions.question: TerramEarth's data science team needs to train ML models on 500TB of vehicle telemetry data weekly. Training takes 8 hours on 100 VMs. They need to minimize cost and avoid paying for idle resources between training runs. Which approach?

choices.text: Vertex AI Custom Training with reserved resources — reserve 100 custom training nodes for the 8-hour window, release after training. More expensive than preemptible but guaranteed availability.
Dataproc Serverless for Spark ML — serverless, auto-scales, pay-per-second for the exact resources used during the 8-hour training. No persistent cluster. Best for variable/batch ML workloads.
Compute Engine with preemptible VMs (100 × preemptible) + startup script to launch training — preemptible costs 91% less but no guaranteed availability, training could be interrupted
Permanent Dataproc cluster (100 nodes) with scheduled scaling — keep 100 nodes running 24/7 even between training runs, very expensive

questions.explanation: Dataproc Serverless for Spark is the modern GCP approach for batch ML workloads: it spins up managed Spark clusters only during job execution, auto-scales during the job, and releases resources when done. Pay-per-second. No cluster management overhead. This eliminates the 91% cost of keeping 100 nodes running between weekly training runs.

questions.question: Helix needs to give external auditors temporary read access to their GCP environment for 30 days. Auditors must not be able to access production data directly. Access must be fully audited. Which approach?

choices.text: Create Google Cloud OAuth 2.0 credentials (client_id/client_secret) for the auditor organization — federate auditor IdP with Cloud Identity, grant Viewer role at org level with conditions limiting to audit log access only
Create dedicated Google accounts for each auditor, assign Viewer role at org level — managing individual Google accounts is operationally heavy, not recommended for temporary access
Use service accounts for auditors — service accounts are for workloads, not humans, and can't be easily restricted to temporary access periods
Enable Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) for the audit dashboard app — IAP is for app-level access, not org-wide read access for auditors

questions.explanation: Cloud Identity (Google's IdP) federates with the auditor's corporate IdP (Azure AD/Okta). Auditors authenticate with their corporate credentials. Time-based Conditional Access policies (30-day expiration) + org-level Viewer role scoped to audit logs and Cloud Audit Logs. Full IAM audit trail. No separate Google accounts needed.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games needs to implement A/B testing for game features: route 20% of players to a new game mode, measure engagement metrics (session length, retention) in real-time, and auto-promote if metrics improve. Which experimentation platform?

choices.text: Cloud Load Balancer with URL map path-based routing (20% to variant) + BigQuery for analytics + manual decision — no automatic promotion, manual analysis required

Firebase Remote Config for feature flags + Firebase Analytics for real-time metrics + Cloud Functions to automate promotion when metrics improve — Firebase handles the experimentation platform end-to-end
Cloud Run with traffic splitting (20% to variant) + Cloud Monitoring dashboards — Cloud Run supports traffic splitting but doesn't provide the experimentation metrics and automatic promotion logic
Spinnaker with canary analysis — Spinnaker has canary analysis but requires significant operational overhead, Firebase Remote Config is purpose-built for feature experimentation

questions.explanation: Firebase Remote Config allows percentage-based feature rollouts. Firebase Analytics tracks engagement metrics in real-time. Cloud Functions (triggered by Analytics conversion events) automatically promotes the variant if metrics improve. This is the Google-recommended A/B testing platform for mobile and game apps.

questions.question: Dress4Win's retail website serves 5 million products from Cloud Storage. SEO requires that image URLs never change (immutable), but they need to migrate from legacy CDN to Cloud CDN. How do you prevent broken image links?

choices.text: Cloud CDN with backend bucket pointing to Cloud Storage + enable cache invalidation for all existing URLs before switch — invalidation clears the old CDN cache, Cloud CDN serves from new origin
Use Cloud Storage object versioning — keep old object names accessible via versioned URLs while new objects use new naming scheme, too complex
Implement a URL forwarding layer (Cloud Load Balancer + URL map) that maps legacy CDN URLs to new Cloud CDN URLs — existing links continue to work via redirect

Cloud CDN with backend bucket (origin is Cloud Storage) + signed URLs with long expiry (1 year) + use object name as immutable identifier (Content-ID or object path) — Cloud CDN caches at edge, Cloud Storage is origin, URLs remain stable

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage object names (URLs) are immutable — once created, the URL never changes. Point Cloud CDN backend bucket to Cloud Storage. Long TTL (1 year) in CDN headers ensures images stay cached. Signed URLs prevent unauthorized access. The migration to Cloud CDN is transparent to existing URLs — just change the DNS record to point to the Cloud CDN IP.

questions.question: TerramEarth's vehicle telemetry is ingested via Pub/Sub and processed by Dataflow. An ML model (deployed to Vertex AI) needs to score each vehicle event in real-time (<500ms end-to-end). Which real-time scoring architecture?

choices.text: Pub/Sub → Dataflow (streaming) → Vertex AI Online Prediction endpoint → Pub/Sub output for scored events — Dataflow calls Vertex AI online prediction for real-time scoring
Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions → Vertex AI Online Prediction — Cloud Functions cold starts make 500ms latency challenging at 500K events/second scale
Pub/Sub Lite (zonal) → Dataflow → Vertex AI batch prediction — batch prediction has hours of latency, not real-time

Pub/Sub → Vertex AI Online Prediction directly (no Dataflow) — Vertex AI online prediction can be called directly but doesn't have the streaming windowing and exactly-once semantics that Dataflow provides

questions.explanation: Dataflow streaming processes vehicle events in real-time, calls Vertex AI Online Prediction for ML scoring, outputs scored events to Pub/Sub for downstream processing. Vertex AI Online Prediction provides sub-100ms prediction latency. Dataflow provides buffering and exactly-once semantics. This is the standard real-time ML scoring pattern on GCP.

questions.question: Helix needs to manage 500 IoT medical devices (heart rate monitors, infusion pumps) that must only communicate with approved GCP services and cannot access the public internet. Devices have limited compute. Which IoT device management and network security?

choices.text: Cloud IoT Core (deprecated) for device registry + authentication via JWT (JSON Web Tokens) + VPC Service Controls to create a data perimeter around Cloud IoT Core and BigQuery

Use Cloud Pub/Sub with service account authentication (JWT) + VPC Service Controls to restrict data exfiltration + Cloud Functions for device command processing — Cloud IoT Core was the native IoT device management but is deprecated
Bare-metal IoT hub on Compute Engine — self-managed IoT hub on GCE, operational overhead, not the recommended approach on GCP
Partner IoT platform (AWS IoT Core or Azure IoT Hub) — wrong cloud, Helix is a GCP-first organization

questions.explanation: Post-IoT Core deprecation, the pattern is: devices authenticate to Pub/Sub using service account JWTs (no keys stored on devices). VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter — devices can only publish to Pub/Sub within the perimeter, cannot exfiltrate data. Cloud Functions (HTTP trigger) handle device commands. Devices with limited compute use lightweight MQTT/HTTP libraries.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games processes 1TB of game replay data daily. Replays are played back for analytics (heatmaps, player movement). Storage format is raw binary (100GB per replay, 10 replays/day). Which storage for both raw replay storage and efficient analytical access?

choices.text: Cloud Storage (Standard) for raw replays + Dataproc Serverless for Spark to process replays into Parquet format + BigQuery for analytics — Dataproc Serverless handles binary file processing efficiently
Cloud Bigtable for replay storage — Bigtable is wide-column, not designed for large binary file storage, would require splitting replays into many rows
Firestore for replay metadata + Cloud Storage for raw replay binary files + Dataproc for analytics — this separates metadata from raw data, metadata in Firestore for fast lookup
Cloud Storage (Nearline) for raw replays + BigQuery with external tables for analytics — external tables can query GCS Parquet but binary raw replays need preprocessing to Parquet first

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage Standard holds the raw binary replays (immutable, cheap storage). Dataproc Serverless (Spark) processes binary replays into analytical format (Parquet) and stores processed analytics data in BigQuery. Dataproc Serverless is the right choice because replay processing is compute-intensive but intermittent (10 replays/day), and Dataproc Serverless eliminates cluster management.

questions.question: Dress4Win's recommendation engine runs on GKE and needs to call a 3rd-party product recommendation API (outside GCP). The API has rate limits (1000 req/min). How do you handle rate limiting and resilience?

choices.text: Deploy Ambassador (Envoy-based API gateway) on GKE for rate limiting + circuit breaker pattern + Redis (Cloud Memorystore) for distributed request counting across pod replicas
Cloud Endpoints with rate limiting enabled — Cloud Endpoints is the GCP-native API gateway for GKE, handles rate limiting and authentication
Cloud Load Balancer with rate limiting — LB rate limiting is at the connection level, not at the API call level, not suitable for per-customer rate limiting
Application-level semaphore with in-memory counter — in-memory counter doesn't sync across pod replicas, race conditions cause incorrect rate limiting

questions.explanation: Ambassador (or similar Envoy-based ingress like GKE Ingress with backend config rate limiting) handles rate limiting at the API gateway layer. Circuit breaker prevents cascade failures when the external API is slow/unavailable. Cloud Memorystore for Redis provides distributed rate limit counters across all pod replicas. This is the standard resilient external API calling pattern.

questions.question: TerramEarth needs to archive 100PB of historical telemetry for 7 years. The legal team requires that archived data cannot be modified or deleted for 7 years (immutability). Which Cloud Storage configuration?

choices.text: Cloud Storage with Object Lock (WORM) in COMPLIANCE mode with 7-year retention + Archive storage class + Cloud KMS for customer-managed encryption keys + lifecycle policy to delete after 7 years
Cloud Storage with Object Versioning enabled + retention policy of 7 years — Object Versioning keeps all versions, retention policy prevents deletion but versioning adds storage cost
Cloud Storage Nearline with 7-year retention policy + Cloud KMS for encryption — Nearline is for frequently accessed data, Archive is for 90+ day access, wrong tier for 7-year archival
Cloud Storage Archive with Object Lock in GOVERNANCE mode (7 years) — GOVERNANCE mode allows bucket管理员 to override retention, not suitable for legal compliance

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode with 7-year retention is legally immutable — even the bucket owner cannot delete objects until the retention period expires. COMPLIANCE mode is required for legal/regulatory compliance. Archive storage class is the cheapest for 7-year cold storage. Cloud KMS CMEK provides encryption with customer-managed keys.

questions.question: Helix's Kubernetes cluster (GKE) stores PHI in Cloud SQL. During a compliance audit, auditors need to verify that all database queries were executed by authorized service accounts. Which audit logging configuration?

choices.text: Enable Cloud Audit Logs (Data Access) for Cloud SQL — Data Access logs record every query including the IAM principal that executed it, export to Cloud Logging log bucket with 7-year retention
Enable Cloud SQL query insights — query insights shows query patterns and performance, not which service account executed which query
Enable Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity only) — Admin Activity logs only record admin actions (create/delete DB), not data access queries
Use Cloud SQL audit logs (PostgreSQL/MySQL native audit logging) — native DB audit logs are stored in the database itself, can be tampered with by DB admins, Cloud Audit Logs are tamper-proof

questions.explanation: Cloud Audit Logs with Data Access (ADMIN_READ and DATA_READ/Data_WRITE) enabled for Cloud SQL records every query with the IAM principal's identity. These logs are written to Cloud Logging (not the database), making them tamper-evident. Export to a dedicated log bucket (in a separate project) with 7-year retention for compliance.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games needs to implement session-based authentication for their game web portal: sessions must persist across device restarts and be valid for 30 days. Which authentication and session management?

choices.text: Firebase Authentication with session cookies (30-day expiry) + Firestore for session metadata (user preferences, game state) — Firebase Auth handles the auth, session cookies persist across devices
Self-managed JWT tokens stored in Cloud Storage — tokens stored as objects in GCS, not secure, requires custom token validation
Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) for authentication — IAP is for GCP resources, not game web portal authentication
GKE Ingress with Cloud Armor for authentication — Cloud Armor is WAF and DDoS protection, not authentication service

questions.explanation: Firebase Authentication (part of Google Cloud) provides the identity layer. Session cookies (JWT-based) with 30-day expiry persist across device restarts. Firestore stores session metadata. This is the managed, scalable approach for game web portal authentication.

questions.question: Dress4Win needs to migrate 20TB of historical order data from an on-premises data warehouse to BigQuery. The data is in SQL Server format with complex joins. Migration must be done in under 4 hours. Which approach?

choices.text: BigQuery Data Transfer Service with on-premises source — BQ DTS supports on-premises SQL Server via Data Transfer Service, handles schema conversion and scheduling
Dataproc (Spark) with connector for SQL Server — Dataproc cluster reads from SQL Server via JDBC, writes to BigQuery via BigQuery connector, parallel transfer handles 20TB in <4 hours
Manual export to CSV, upload to Cloud Storage, load to BigQuery — manual process is too slow and error-prone for 20TB in 4 hours
Cloud Data Fusion with JDBC connector — Data Fusion is ETL orchestration, Dataproc is the faster choice for bulk transfer from SQL Server to BigQuery

questions.explanation: Dataproc with Spark (BigQuery connector) is the fastest way to bulk transfer 20TB from SQL Server to BigQuery: parallel JDBC reads from SQL Server, parallel writes to BigQuery. A Dataproc cluster with 20 workers can transfer 20TB in under 4 hours. BQ DTS doesn't natively support on-premises SQL Server.

questions.question: TerramEarth's data pipeline uses Dataflow streaming. During a deployment, a bug caused 1 hour of vehicle telemetry data to be incorrectly processed (all speed values were multiplied by 0.1). The data has already been written to BigQuery. How do you fix this?

choices.text: Use BigQuery Time Travel (up to 7 days) to query the pre-deployment state of the table, identify affected partitions, re-process from Pub/Sub replay with corrected pipeline, overwrite affected partitions
Update all affected rows manually with UPDATE statements in BigQuery — 1 hour of data at 500K messages/sec = billions of rows, too slow and expensive
Delete the affected BigQuery partition and re-run the Dataflow job with the fixed code — this requires re-processing all data through Dataflow which may be slow, but is correct
BigQuery Time Travel can't help — Time Travel only shows historical data, can't be used to restore to a specific point. Use Cloud SQL with point-in-time recovery instead.

questions.explanation: BigQuery Time Travel (available up to 7 days) allows querying the table as it existed before the buggy deployment. Use Time Travel to identify the affected time window, then replay Pub/Sub messages (retained for 7 days) through the corrected Dataflow pipeline, and overwrite the affected BigQuery partitions. This is the standard BigQuery data repair pattern.

questions.question: Helix needs to grant a 3rd-party auditor temporary (30-day) access to read specific BigQuery tables containing de-identified clinical trial results. The auditor uses their own identity provider (Okta). How do you enable this?

choices.text: Set up Cloud Identity (Google Workspace) federation with Okta as the IdP (SAML 2.0) + create a Google Group in Cloud Identity + add auditor's Okta users to the group + grant BigQuery Data Viewer IAM role to the group with Condition: access expire in 30 days
Create service account credentials for the auditor — service accounts are for workloads, not human auditors, and keys can't be easily expired in 30 days
Share BigQuery datasets via public sharing — sharing with the internet is a major security risk, not acceptable for PHI data
Export data to Cloud Storage and share via signed URLs — exporting PHI outside BigQuery violates the data perimeter and HIPAA requirements

questions.explanation: Cloud Identity federation with Okta lets auditors authenticate with their existing Okta credentials. IAM Conditional Access with expiration (accessPolicies/conditions with timestampBefore) automatically revokes access after 30 days. BigQuery Data Viewer role (dataset or table level) limits access to the specific tables. Group membership in Cloud Identity makes it easy to add/remove auditors.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games processes player transactions (in-game purchases) through a microservices architecture on GKE. Transaction data must be written to BigQuery for financial reporting. At-least-once delivery is acceptable. How do you ensure exactly-once financial reporting?

choices.text: Pub/Sub (at-least-once) → Dataflow (exactly-once with deduplication) → BigQuery with INSERT ALL (merge) — Dataflow uses shuffle-backed state to deduplicate within a window, INSERT ALL merges on transaction_id to prevent duplicates in BigQuery
Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery enabled → Dataflow → BigQuery streaming insert — Pub/Sub exactly-once is sufficient, Dataflow and BigQuery can be at-least-once
Pub/Sub → Dataflow with windowed aggregation → BigQuery — windowing aggregates data, doesn't guarantee exactly-once for individual transaction records
Pub/Sub Lite (zonal) → Cloud Functions → BigQuery — Cloud Functions doesn't provide exactly-once semantics, at-least-once only

questions.explanation: Dataflow's exactly-once processing uses shuffle-backed state for deduplication. Combined with BigQuery INSERT ALL (merge) using transaction_id as the key ensures duplicates are merged in BigQuery. This is the financial-grade exactly-once pattern on GCP.

questions.question: Dress4Win's GKE cluster stores session state in an in-memory HashMap in each pod. During peak traffic, sessions are lost when pods restart due to scaling. Which stateful session management solution?

choices.text: Cloud Memorystore for Redis — in-memory Redis store, sub-millisecond latency, deploy a Redis cluster (2 shards, 1 replica each) for HA, session affinity via sticky sessions on the load balancer
GKE with StatefulSet and PersistentVolumeClaim — StatefulSets are for stateful workloads like databases, not for session caching, adds unnecessary complexity
Firestore for session storage — Firestore is multi-region, persistent, but has higher latency (10-100ms) than Redis for session cache use case
GCS for session persistence — GCS is object storage, not designed for high-frequency session read/write, unsuitable for session cache

questions.explanation: Cloud Memorystore for Redis (HA with automatic failover) is the standard GKE session management solution: sub-millisecond latency, fully managed with automatic failover, compatible with Redis session provider libraries in all major web frameworks. StatefulSets are for stateful databases, not ephemeral session cache.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games' real-time game analytics (Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery) experienced a 30-minute data gap due to a Dataflow job failure. What should they implement to prevent this?

choices.text: Configure Pub/Sub to retain messages for 7 days + Dataflow with stateful processing and checkpointing + restart from last checkpoint on failure
Increase Dataflow worker count to prevent job failure — more workers don't prevent all job failures
Use Dataflow with a dead-letter queue for failed messages — DLQ handles failures after they happen, not prevention
Configure Pub/Sub with exactly-once delivery — exactly-once prevents duplicates but doesn't help with job failure recovery

questions.explanation: Dataflow's stateful processing with checkpointing allows the job to resume from the last successful checkpoint. Pub/Sub's 7-day retention allows replay from the gap start time. Together these prevent data loss during job failures.

questions.question: Dress4Win's containerized recommendation engine (GKE) needs to call an external 3rd-party API that requires mutual TLS (mTLS). The API provider requires client certificate authentication. Which is the most secure way to implement this?

choices.text: Store client certificate in Secret Manager, retrieve at pod startup via Workload Identity (GCP Service Account), pass to the application — Workload Identity eliminates key management and keys never in etcd
Store the client certificate and private key in Kubernetes Secret, mount as a volume in the pod — simplest approach but keys are stored in etcd (encrypted at rest)
Use Istio service mesh with PeerAuthentication policy set to mTLS STRICT — Istio handles cert rotation but requires managing the Istio control plane
Use Cloud HSM to generate and store the client certificate — HSM is for key generation, the certificate still needs to be provisioned to the pod

questions.explanation: Secret Manager + Workload Identity is the most secure approach: keys are never stored in Kubernetes. The pod uses Workload Identity to authenticate to Secret Manager, retrieves the certificate at startup. No secrets in etcd, no manual key rotation needed.

questions.question: TerramEarth's vehicles are in areas with intermittent satellite connectivity (30 seconds connected, 5 minutes disconnected). They need to batch sensor data locally and upload when connected. Data must not be lost. Which edge storage and sync architecture?

choices.text: Vehicle gateway runs a local MQTT broker (Mosquitto) for buffering, batches upload to Pub/Sub via HTTP when connected — MQTT provides reliable local buffering during disconnection periods
Vehicle uses HTTP/REST POST to Cloud Pub/Sub directly when connected — no local buffering, data lost when offline
Use Cloud IoT Edge running on the vehicle gateway with local Pub/Sub Lite for buffering — IoT Edge is deprecated
Use Cloud Storage with resumable uploads — Cloud Storage resumable uploads handle intermittent connectivity but don't provide local buffering and state management

questions.explanation: The vehicle gateway runs a local MQTT broker for local buffering during disconnection. When connectivity is available, it uploads batched messages to Pub/Sub via HTTP. This pattern handles intermittent connectivity while guaranteeing no data loss.

questions.question: Helix needs to run a clinical trial analysis app on GKE that accesses PHI from Cloud SQL. A 3rd-party vendor needs temporary access to debug a production issue. How do you enable this securely?

choices.text: Create a temporary service account for the vendor, assign minimal RBAC, set Service Account Key Expiration to 24 hours using IAM Conditions, enable Cloud Audit Logs, revoke after issue resolution
Add the vendor's Google account as an Owner on the project — too broad, full access to everything
Share the existing service account credentials via Secret Manager with the vendor — sharing credentials is a security risk regardless of storage method
Use Cloud IAP to tunnel to the GKE cluster — IAP is for human access, not for a 3rd-party application debugging a service

questions.explanation: Create a dedicated debug service account with minimal permissions. Set IAM Condition for serviceAccountKeyExpiresAfter=86400s. Enable Cloud Audit Logs to track access. Immediately revoke after resolution. This is the GCP recommended pattern for temporary vendor access.

questions.question: Mountkirk Games wants to implement a game moderation system: player chat messages are processed in real-time, and toxic content is flagged and deleted within 5 seconds. Which GCP service for real-time content moderation?

choices.text: Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions (trigger on message) → Cloud Natural Language API (moderateContent) → if toxic, publish to Pub/Sub topic to delete the message from the game server
Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery for storage → scheduled review — too slow for 5-second moderation requirement
Cloud Armor for content moderation — Cloud Armor is WAF/DDoS protection, not content moderation for chat
Vertex AI TextModeration API — Vertex AI has content moderation but Cloud NL API's moderateContent is the purpose-built API for this

questions.explanation: Cloud Natural Language API's moderateContent endpoint is purpose-built for real-time content moderation. Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions → NL API → Pub/Sub for deletion provides sub-5-second latency for chat moderation. This is the simplest, fully managed GCP-native approach.
